Okay, so let's talk sort of around copywriting. Oh, thank you very much. Give the old man his first, but I am going to want one. So, sort of the first thing you and I agreed that we would talk about is some underlying fundamental insight into the psychology of the buyer. The person we are actually trying to get to read our copy, respond to our copy, buy things from us, etc. So, what's your overarching position on who this person is and sort of where they start? Okay, first, is anybody mad at me from last night? What do you care? Just Deutsch. So, as anybody got, as anybody spent time studying psychology, got a degree in it or just dabbled in a pop psychology, anybody hung out with old school street-wise salesman? That would be the main thing. Really? So, okay. Well, I accidentally got a... Well, let me give you a better one. No, I'll help you. So, how many people in here have actually sold hardcore nose-to-nose, toes-to-toes, on the living room floor, in a showroom, etc. Okay. Now, keep your hands up if you actually cashed a commission check. I mean, you actually made some money doing it. Okay, see, they're not hopeless. Okay, good. There you go. Yeah, most... I accidentally got a degree in psychology, a four-year degree from the University of California, and I graduated into the teeth of the 74 Recession, and absolutely nothing I learned there was of any value. There were 20,000 BAs in psychology, given out of the United States that year, for one job that was available. I think I wanted to be a childcare worker in Sacramento for a couple of years. Anyway... Did you know, by the way, that was on the test you take in high school, I was supposed to be a social worker? Oh, really? Yeah. That was... I'm serious. In a way, you know. No, I'm serious. That's what the... You know that aptitude test thing you take? I was supposed to be a social... Hey, I'd have been efficient. I could handle a big case load. Get a job. Next! I mean, you know... Stop whining, yeah. So I took an interest in psychology into my life as a freelance copywriter and hung out with the street-wise salesman, and really paid attention to old-school psychology. So when I started writing direct response advertising, it was a very short move for me to go from looking at the audiences out there in a target market, and then looking kind of beneath the surface, and looking at what I believed they needed to happen to be able to move them off of their inaction. And when I described it later in... When I started writing about this, I described people in the average market as a synambulistic blob welded to the couch so firmly it wouldn't leave to save its ass if the house was burning down. And your job as an ad-rivers, you get them to take out their wallet and actually take some action. So this is what I was talking about last night with how easy it can be to get somebody up on the fence. To get somebody to agree with you that, yeah, you got a good product, and maybe someday down the road I'll buy it. But you've got to get them to act, and that's where the pro is separated from the rookie. You know, that's why... And I'm reasonably certain you agree. That's why I push people to study the old direct response so much. I say there's old and the gold, because they had a much tougher job at what he just described really than we do now. Because they actually did have to get them up off the couch. I mean, somebody had to go get a checkbook out of the kitchen drawer, write out a check, fill out a coupon, put it in an envelope, not talk themselves out of it, get a postage stamp, and drop it in a mailbox in order to respond. And then finally, they were able to respond by phone when 800 numbers and the ability to take a credit card over the phone. But that's actually... That's more recent than it is ancient. Sugarman was the first guy to put an 800 number in a direct response ad and be able to take orders by credit card over the phone. So that's what? 30... Early 70s. Yeah, early 70s. So I know for some of you that's ancient history, but not for John and I, and not for many. I mean, it's not like it was at the turn of the century for God's sakes. So those people doing direct response then had a much higher hurdle to move this person, John just described. And I generally share the same disdainful idea of where they are. Well, there's a subtle balance there. It's not... Don't get the idea that I don't like my customer. I like him a lot. In fact, one of the things that I try to teach people to come to me for advice just on life and stuff, is that salesmen lead better lives. The people who know how to sell and understand what Dan and I are talking about, the direct... The ability to get somebody to move and actually buy something, you lead a better life because you can't do that if you don't live firmly in reality. And the vast majority of people live kind of on the surface of life, kind of bobbing around like flotsam and jetsam on the surface of the ocean, taking hither and to, by the waves. And entrepreneurs and who are good salesmen actually start to control your life. You have goals, you go forward, you are the engine of your success. You move yourself forward. That is not shared by the vast majority of people out there. They are living in very shallow lives. Salesmen lead better lives all the way around. You don't look at the world the way you wish it was or the way you think it ought to be. You look at the way the world, the way it is. And people get shocked when they first start thinking about this because they take a look. Does that mean I have to despise my customer base? No. You actually start to love people more when all of the nonsense falls away. All of the BS is taken away. And you are actually dealing with people for who they are and how they will actually act as opposed to the way you think they should or ought to act. And it changes the whole dynamic. And where they start. So I think that one of the mistakes, and so salespeople, you learn not to start at a different place than the prospect is because you get feedback. You sit across the kitchen table, you sit across the desk in the office, and if they're over here and you start here, you pretty quickly get a feel of that and you sort of back up and retrench. We don't get that feedback when we sell through media. And a very common mistake is making assumptions about where they are and starting further along than they are in the process towards making a decision about whatever it is that you are putting in front of them. So like what you just said, that we have our own engines and our engines start pretty quick. They either don't have their own engine or it's still got a hand crank. And so we got to start way back with them there, not like ready to hear the good news about the thing we have to offer them. And Dan, do you remember the first time you met somebody who was a customer who you had not met before and they were a little astonished that you didn't know them because they felt they knew you so well. Yes. They actually felt they had. Which is what's supposed to happen, right? But that moment that happens is you realize now you've arrived as a marketer because the communication that you just affected with them might have been a year ago and they bought and then you've got more things going back and forth. They're reading your newsletters, they're reading your stuff. They feel that connection on a visceral level. And how many people feel you know Dan after reading them these years before you met them? So that's a major step in moving from being a social entrepreneur to having the unlimited potential. And I'd like to add one other side to that psychology thing that I had. For myself, I operated from a gun to the head position. And I found out later that Halbert did the same thing and J.A. Abraham did the same thing. I don't know if you did. But I had no backup. I had no freelance. I'd never met a freelancer when I became one. I was making up the gig as I was going along. And I was one of the few freelancers down in Los Angeles in the 80s because it just wasn't an occupation that was well thought of or even that a lot of people knew about. So I had to make up things as I was going. One of the things I did was I imagined a gun was to my head when I was writing an ad that would go off if the ad didn't work. And how would I react to that? So would I choose that headline? Would I take that tact in my sales pitch? Would I tell a joke or would I be straightforward? And over and over again, that idea of my life is on the line if this ad doesn't work kept me really focused on classic salesmanship. So I didn't experiment because when it came down to it, writing for a client, I already had the money or I was going to get paid whether the ad works or not. So in a lot of writers, you can get lazy. Rather than that, I kept thinking what's the one thing I would do if my life really, really depended on that. So the gun to the head philosophy of working erases a lot of the BS that can leak into your writing when you think, well, why not try this? Well, because it won't work. One of the evils of the Internet is that it has caused marketers to dramatically increase the quantity of their output to their customers and made them, therefore, quick, sloppy and casual about a lot of it. Whereas really, most people would be better off putting 75% less out and really working on the 25% to get it right. I mean, John's absolutely right. I mean, those of us, and of course, once as a freelancer, once you're in the game for royalties, not just fees, then from a monetary standpoint, you actually care whether or not this stuff works, not just from an ethical standpoint or a professional reputation standpoint. But I'm often more concerned that it works than the client is, even. And yeah, you work on it like this is life or death and this baby's got to produce or else. And I think a lot of marketers do not do that. They are like, okay, we're going to get two emails out a day this week and we're going to do it every day for five days. Let's slap these babies together and let's push the button. And I think it's a mistake. And one of the things that I know you've come up against with clients, which I do, is that you would write the ad that you believe needs to be run to do it. And everything that you know says this ad needs to be run. What's the first thing the client says? My daughter, the English lit major, read it and thinks you ought to change the headline now. He says this word and do this. And if you don't have control as a freelance, one of the first things I teach freelancers is to become the adult in the room. You control the situation. You are not a vendor. You are in control. You do that by charging massive amounts of money. And firing people. And one of the things I used to do in the agency is I'd walk in and I said, I won't work with you guys until you fire the designer. I had way too much seeing what the ad looked like. He wanted gray floating blocks of coffee under his designs. But those back in the 80s before Direct Response had re-established itself. Right. See, John, the chief point here is that there are reliable things to do. And they are based on behavioral. So I say Direct Response is roughly half behavioral psych and half math. That's really what it is. And so there's reliable psychological principles. There's reliable sales principles. And yes, somebody periodically innovates, but it's really not necessary to be the innovator in order to make a lot of money in this field. There is a fairly predictable, reliable path of elements that make things work. And they really haven't changed. Media's changed, technology's changed, you know, but a presentation that works is a presentation that works is a presentation that works. And you and I, as writers, what were we just talking about in the hallway before we came in? If we want to be creative, we'll go right on a freaking novel. We'll write an album. And be creative there. Although it turns out, truth be told, so that's not that creative a process either. So, I mean, when I got an advertising, I thought it was a creative exercise, you know, and it is not that at all. It is a methodical exercise. It turns out fiction is harder. Yes, harder and also methodical. There are formulas. There are architectures. There are reliable things. I want to shift gears because I'm going to watch the clock. So, I'd like you to talk a little bit about the whole circle the desk thing. Sure. What goes on really before we get a word on paper? I did two things when I started writing for myself. And really, being a freelancer is an entrepreneurial gig. It was my own business. And I had to figure out how to make sure I was doing it and doing it right. So, I made a couple of decisions. One was business before pleasure, which for a slacker changed my life. That's all I did is I wasn't going to, I could still have a drink with the boys and party and do whatever, but I had to finish all the business first. So, that was huge. But then what I did is I started thinking, wait a minute, I need to really nail down this working environment for me. I needed every trick in the book on my side. So, I actually wore writing clothes. How many people have writing clothes or a hat or something that they do? Okay, you know, you've heard people talk about put on your writer hat or something or put on your salesman hat. I literally did that and it helped. Now, one of the reasons it helped, excuse me, one of the reasons that helped is because my writing clothes are dirty sweats in an old t-shirt. So, I actually couldn't go to the door and answer it or walk outside to the thing. I was stuck in there unless I was going to change clothes. And I actually had different hats on that I would do. I'd put a different hat on when I would call a client. I'd put a different hat on when I was ready to sit down and actually face the, I would start out with a typewriter. That's how old I freaking am. But, you know, whether I was standing on a monitor or a blank sheet of paper. And what I developed, what Dan was mentioning there is this habit of getting into the mood to write. How many people, I told you that writer's block doesn't exist last night, but how many people have trouble gearing up to sit down and actually write stuff you need to write? Yeah, it's because your subconscious isn't ready. Right. That's exactly... And that's why what I started doing is, I like dogs. I noticed that dogs circle things, you know, they circle around before they lay down. And I started circling the desk and I'd actually worked myself into a froth. Now, I didn't approach the desk until I was ready to write. And that's why I never encountered writer's block. So what Dan was talking about last night, I had all my ducks in a row. I knew what I was going to do. So when I sat down, I wasn't thinking, well, where do I start? I knew where to start. I knew who I was going after. I knew what the pain points were. I knew what the feature benefits were. And by the way, when I write, I usually write the bullets first and then move to a headline and then start writing. So I construct the ad in a kind of like building a house or remodeling a house. So, but I would circle the desk and work myself into a froth. And one of the things that I thought when I was sitting down, I would think, God damn it, there are people walking around out there today that have money in their wallet that belongs to me. And the universe will not be right until I rectify that situation. Thank you very much. Dogs also pee to mark their territory. I hope you were not doing that in your office. Dan, you promised you wouldn't bring that part of the work. I wear astronaut underwear when I write. That work area probably got smelly after a length of time. So I hear from people all the time, your hands went up a lot, that, you know, for you writing is hard and oh, how can I... Newsletter once a month or saleslet. If writing and writing at a decent speed is difficult for you, it is because your preparation is poor. That's just that simple. And one or both kinds of preparation. So one is the pragmatic preparation of knowing who you're... Having a profile of who you're writing to, having some sort of template in place, having some good swipe files until you get so old and accomplished at it like he and I are where I imagine you like me now. 90% of the time you're working out of the swipe files in your head, not the swipe files in the back room, although they are still there. But so there's that kind of preparation, you know, am I practically ready to write? And then there's, is your subconscious in gear, does it know that that's what's supposed to be happening right now? And almost every copywriter has some sort of ritualistic behavior, not necessarily the one he described, but some sort of ritualistic behavior. They have an environment, I mean real copywriters aren't writing at Starbucks. The world thinks that's their office somehow, and Schultz has convinced everybody that that's their office or Panera Bread for God's sakes. Real copywriters have an environment that they have organized, a place, and not only is it because their stuff is there and they're maybe protected from disruptions, it's because their subconscious knows when they enter that place, oh okay, I gotta flip open the drawers, I gotta turn on the switches. This is now all about writing. And so when it's difficult for you, it's really a matter of preparation. More than it is you can't write, writing is hard, you don't have a good idea. Let me help some of you out who do have this trouble. Just go ahead and develop your own routines, you can do whatever you want. There are two kinds of writers, those who work in complete silence, I work in complete silence, and those who play music in the background. Stephen King wrote with ACVC, Blaring, all that. I have a TV on. Okay, so there's a number of writers. I'm over here, and I don't see it. Exactly, so it's like white noise, so don't worry about that. Choose either one or the other, but get your routine down so that you do that. Take the phone off the hook, and make a big deal about taking the phone off the hook. Nobody has hooks. Pardon me? Phone off hook, typewriter. Thanks for the ad. Actually, I had a wooden chair. I'm sensitive to this, because I have this titanium member who has no frame of reference for any of this stuff. So like 20 times during a mastermind meeting, I have to explain to her what the object is I'm talking about, because, you know. The color adjustment knob on the table. Exactly, yeah, exactly. So get your, get your, God damn, I hadn't thought about that in a long time. A color adjustment, wow. Shut up. Wow. I found a picture of myself in the 50s, and the first TV you had was round, you know, and that was a move up from the one that reflected off the mirror when you opened, anyway. What was I talking about? Oh, and for your routine, whatever you need to do, but get the routine down, it can be a two minute routine or a five minute routine, whatever you do, but set it down. But make it so that nobody's going to disturb you, and then set a clock. This was a big break for a lot of people. This is big. This is very big. Yeah, work, start with 50 minutes out of an hour, work for two hours with 10 minute breaks. So work for 50 minutes, and then take a 10 minute break and literally leave, get out of that, and when you come back, do your routine again to get back into it. We're stylistically different on that. So like I take no breaks. But, find out. Right, but I write to a deadline just like you do. I mean, if you don't have a stop point, if you don't have a stop point that you're working toward, your subconscious just doesn't know when it needs to be done. It's astonishing what an alarm clock, and I would get an old school alarm clock, you know, or you can use your iPhone and it goes off and it does that little drum beat or whatever. See, now you're making adjustments. See, I made you hypersensitive about that. Very, very, very true. Actually, I have a dog that wakes up and has to go pee every hour. There you go, perfect. But find out, and it could be your 45 minutes and 15 minutes, could be your half an hour and half an hour, could be, mine's more like two hours and then half an hour, and then back for two hours, and then I'm done. Four hours of writing is more, if you're steady productive in four hours of writing, you get more done than the average marketer gets done in a month. But you want to dictate end times, and I dictate the amount of work that's going to be done by that end time. So if it's 16 pages, and it's been assigned 50 minutes, my entire system now knows that it's got 50 minutes to do 16 pages. And one of the big things I remember, the use cemented down, I was kind of already in that camp, but I tend to be a perfectionist sometimes. I know it's hard to believe, but sometimes I do. And Dan used to preach, good enough is good enough. I don't know if you already talked about it yesterday or not, but that is really important. No win to stop, and in short, it's not perfect. Perfect dad seldom work anyway. And often what you're doing is you're messing with what's going to be a 1% difference. That's right, it's incremental. So when it's good enough, that's when you pump. That's why Dan does two edits and he's done. I will do more, but I'm working sometimes for clients who demand that perfection. They don't want the occasional typo in there, but most ads, we used to purposely put typos in our printed ads and our direct mail. So if it was perfect, it still didn't look perfect, but you can get that just by doing two less edits usually. Okay, that's a tangent. So last night, I know you lived on USP. However, maybe to revisit or extend or give you another bite at that Apple. I think one-legged golfers courses, an all-time classic. The process of getting there? Yeah, I think you're one of the best at getting to the big idea, what Nicholas called the hidden benefit, the hook, the thing that really makes a pitch stand apart from all other pitches in its category. Okay, how many people have seen my one-legged offer ad? That was a big breakthrough for me because of two things. One was I had a client who I wasn't going to work for. I was actually having my second or third midlife crisis and was playing in biker bars in this band that I formed. I didn't want to work at all. So they lured me to come back to work. They promised they would take the leash off. What that meant was that they were going to run anything I wrote. They would mail it and they would put it into magazines and they'd swallow hard and they would not change a word. That to a writer who has worked with clients who has always changed his stuff was like catnip. It was just okay, so we didn't get it. So I just threw myself into this knowing that there was no limit to what I could do. I could ruin these guys if I wanted to, but my goal was to make them very successful, which I was able to do. So what I did is what Dan was talking about yesterday, again, the prep part. I started interviewing clients. One of the first products they had was a golf guy who had this strange swing that if you adopted the swing and they were selling videotapes. That's going back, not even DVDs. On videotape, they just filmed this guy doing this swing and I was supposed to sell this videotape, an instructional golf video. It was kind of new back then. This is probably 1991, I think something. So I thought, that's pretty boring. There's not a lot going on there. There's nothing for me to get my teeth around. So I interviewed what we called the talent, the guy behind the thing. I said, I was talking to him for about an hour on the phone. Wacky guy, but no stories came out. And finally I said, how did you come up with this triple coil swing that you call? He says, ah, that old story. Nobody wants to hear that. I was golfing one day behind a four-sum. One of the guys only had one leg. And as he hopped up to the first tee to swing, I thought for sure he was going to fall over. But he didn't. He had this perfect swing and he knocked a 300-yard drive. And I had this epiphany at the point and I just said, stop. And I said, you know, I pretty much got it. So this is what inspired him. He says, yeah, but nobody wants to hear that stuff. Here's what's really important about this, because we have this experience with clients all the time. They've got something spectacular. And it's old hat to them. They don't know it has any value, which means you can't get it.
